title: Vector Database (Generic)
description: Generic base pattern for vector/embedding databases used in AI/ML applications for similarity search. Stores high-dimensional vectors and performs nearest-neighbor queries. Common for RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation), semantic search, and recommendation systems. Choose a specific variant (Pinecone, pgvector) for implementation.

provides.reasoning: Core capability of vector databases - storing high-dimensional embeddings/vectors for AI/ML applications. This is semantically accurate and appropriately specific.

Essential capability for vector databases - performing nearest-neighbor/similarity searches across stored vectors. This is the primary query mechanism that distinguishes vector databases from traditional databases.
Commonly used terminology in AI/ML contexts for databases that store and query vector embeddings. Synonymous with vector-storage but more specific to the embedding use case.
More technical variant of similarity-search that specifically describes the k-NN algorithm used for finding similar vectors. Essential for precision in describing vector database capabilities.
Approximate Nearest Neighbor search is the specific algorithmic approach used by production vector databases for efficient similarity search at scale.
Vector indexing algorithms (HNSW, IVF, etc.) are key differentiators of vector databases, enabling efficient search operations over large vector collections.
Vector databases integrate with embedding models and services for RAG use cases, storing and retrieving embeddings generated by LLMs or other AI models.

requires.reasoning: Vector databases need underlying persistent storage to maintain vector data and indexes. Essential for durability and production use.

Similarity search operations (cosine similarity, euclidean distance calculations, ANN algorithms) are computationally intensive, requiring compute resources for vector comparisons and index maintenance.
Production vector databases commonly support distributed/multi-node deployments for scalability and high availability, requiring network connectivity for cluster coordination and replication.

distance_metric.description: Distance metric for similarity calculation. Cosine (normalized) is standard for embeddings, euclidean for spatial data, dot_product for pre-normalized vectors, manhattan for high-dimensional sparse vectors.

vector_dimensions.description: Dimensionality of vectors stored. Must match embedding model output (1536 for OpenAI text-embedding-ada-002, 768 for sentence-transformers, 384 for MiniLM).

index_type.description: Index algorithm trade-offs: HNSW (fast queries, high memory), IVF (balanced, requires training), flat (exact results, slow at scale).

enable_metadata_filtering.description: Allow filtering vectors by metadata fields before similarity search. Essential for multi-tenant or filtered RAG applications but adds storage overhead.

replication_factor.description: Number of data replicas for high availability. 1 for dev/cost savings, 2+ for production resilience. Increases storage costs linearly.

service_auth_method.description: Vector database connection authentication method. Most managed vector DBs use API keys; self-hosted variants may use password or mTLS.

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Vector database costs vary widely by provider and scale. Min ($25) represents starter tier for development/small apps: Pinecone Starter ($70/mo but prorated ~$25 for minimal usage), Qdrant Cloud basic tier, or self-hosted pgvector on small PostgreSQL instance (t3.small ~$25/mo). Max ($5000) represents production scale: Pinecone Enterprise with millions of vectors and high query volume ($500-2000/mo), Weaviate Cloud high-performance cluster ($1000-3000/mo), or managed vector DB with high-dimensional embeddings (1536 dims for OpenAI, 768 for others) serving thousands of queries/second. Costs scale with: vector count (millions vs billions), dimensions (128-1536+), query throughput (QPS), index types (HNSW, IVF), storage (hot vs cold), and replication. Mid-range production typically $200-800/mo.
provenance.adoptionCost: Medium-high complexity implementation. Requires understanding of: vector embeddings and dimensionality concepts (OpenAI, Cohere, custom models), similarity metrics (cosine, euclidean, dot product), indexing algorithms (HNSW, IVF-PQ, LSH), chunking strategies for RAG applications, metadata filtering, and performance tuning. Development includes: choosing embedding model, data pipeline for vectorization, ingestion workflows, query optimization, testing similarity thresholds, integration with LLM workflows. Most teams need 1-2 weeks for initial setup and 1-2 weeks for optimization. Learning curve moderate but requires ML/AI knowledge. AI assistants can generate boilerplate code but domain expertise needed for proper architecture decisions around chunking size, overlap, and retrieval strategies.
provenance.licenseCost: Multiple open-source options available with zero licensing costs: pgvector (PostgreSQL extension), Qdrant (Apache 2.0), Milvus (Apache 2.0), Weaviate (BSD), ChromaDB (Apache 2.0), and FAISS (MIT). Managed SaaS offerings (Pinecone, Qdrant Cloud, Weaviate Cloud) operate on usage-based pricing without separate license fees. Self-hosted deployments incur only infrastructure costs. Enterprise support contracts optional but not required for basic functionality. No proprietary licensing barriers for vector database technology.

supports_nfr.reason: Vector databases can achieve up to 99.9% availability with multi-region managed deployments.
Vector similarity search requires at least 10ms p95 latency due to high-dimensional ANN computation.
Vector databases require at least 50ms p99 latency for complex ANN queries with large result sets.
Minimum RTO of 5 minutes achievable with automated failover in managed vector database services.
Vendor like Pinecone supports up to 360M vector operations per hour as throughput ceiling.
Vector databases support up to 1000 read QPS for similarity search queries under typical configurations.
Vector databases support up to 10K write QPS for embedding insertion and update operations.
Vector databases support tenant isolation via namespaces/collections (schema-per-tenant) or row-level security.
Vector databases can store embeddings of PII data or work with anonymized vectors depending on use case.
Vector databases support GDPR compliance with data deletion and access controls but it is optional.
Vector databases can be configured for HIPAA compliance with encryption and audit logging but it is optional.
Vector databases support CCPA compliance with data access and deletion capabilities but it is optional.
Vector databases can support SOX compliance with audit trails and immutability but it is optional.
Vector databases provide read-your-writes consistency for single-client sessions but can work without it.
Vector databases support strict durability with write-ahead logs and persistence but can operate without it.

supports_constraints.reason: Vector database pattern requires vector_search feature to be enabled as this is the core capability.

requires_constraints.reason: Vector databases can be deployed on any cloud provider, on-premises, or as cloud-agnostic managed services.
Vector databases are infrastructure components accessed via client SDKs available for all major languages.
Vector databases support backend platforms performing embedding operations including API services and ML workloads.
Vector search results may be cached for performance but caching is not required for vector database functionality.
Vector databases can integrate with async messaging for embedding pipelines but work without it.
Vector databases are commonly used with AI inference for RAG and semantic search but can be used standalone.
Vector databases support multi-tenancy via namespaces and collections but can also be used for single-tenant applications.
Vector databases support batch insertion of embeddings and batch similarity queries but also handle individual operations.
Vector databases typically do not require distributed transactions for similarity search operations.
Vector databases can integrate with real-time streaming for continuous embedding updates but work without it.
Vector databases often store metadata and documents alongside vectors but can work with vectors only.
Vector databases may provide key-value lookups for vector IDs and metadata but this is optional.
Vector databases focus on similarity search rather than graph traversal but some support hybrid capabilities.
Vector databases can store temporal embeddings for time-series analysis but are not primarily time-series databases.
Vector databases support transactional writes for embedding updates but are primarily optimized for search workloads.
Vector databases can support analytical queries over embedding spaces but are not primarily OLAP systems.

Vector similarity search requires at least 10ms p95 latency due to high-dimensional ANN computation.
Vector databases require at least 50ms p99 latency for complex ANN queries with large result sets.
Minimum RTO of 5 minutes achievable with automated failover in managed vector database services.
Vendor like pgvector and Pinecone supports near-zero RPO with proper configuration..
Vendor like Pinecone supports up to 360M vector operations per hour as throughput ceiling.
Vector databases support up to 1000 read QPS for similarity search queries under typical configurations.
